coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous adsorption of hydrophilic and lipophilic dyes on iron-doped hydroxyapatite/carbon dots nanocomposites

coredata.dc:description: Nanocomposites of iron-doped hydroxyapatite and carbon dots were synthesized through green synthesis processes, using Yerba Mate (Ilex paraguariensis) as a precursor. A thorough characterization of the particles was carried out by HRTEM, XRD, FTIR, DLS, XPD, and static contact angle. The characterization indicated that the crystallinity, surface charge, and wettability depend on the synthesis parameters. Carbon dots-doped materials, YMnAp@CD and YMFenAp@CD, showed amphiphilic properties with the capacity to adsorb Crystal violet (CV) and Oil Blue A (OBA) from aqueous or toluene-based solutions. The mentioned materials showed the highest number of adsorption sites, with values of 2.95 ± 0.05 and 3.18 ± 0.04 mmol CV/gads and 1.65 ± 0.02 0.97 ± 0.06 mmol OBA/gads on monocomponent systems, and through a multi-component analysis, the competition for adsorption sites was elucidated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM micrographs of (from left to right) Top: FenAp, YMnAp, and YMFenAp and Bottom: YMnAp@CD and YMFenAp@CD.
Vertically offset XRD diffractograms of the obtained materials. The vertical lines stand for the pure AP theoretical pattern.
FTIR spectra of (from bottom to top): nAp, YMnAp, FenAp, YMFenAp, YMnAp@CD and YMFenAp@CD. Some vibrational modes are shown in the figure for a better understanding.
Electrophoretic mobility profiles according to pH of all materials suspended in water with KNO3 as background electrolyte, measured at (25.0 ± 0.1) °C.

Crystal violet (top) and oil blue A (OBA) adsorption isotherms at (25 ± 1)°C, onto the different materials. The organic molecule of the corresponding dye is shown in the corresponding graphic. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Flow chart of the synthesis pathways used for obtaining all the material.

SBET : Surface area obtained by N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms through the BET model, SCA: Static contact angle of the obtained materials in gas–liquid-solid systems composed of air, water, or toluene and the nanomaterial. µe /H2O: Electrophoretic mobility of an aqueous suspension of the material in pH = 7.0. Surface composition: Surface stoichiometric ratio obtained by XPS Analysis.
Fitting parameters values of the one-site adsorption Langmuir isotherm model.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanocomposites of iron-doped hydroxyapatite and carbon dots were synthesized through green synthesis processes, using Yerba Mate (Ilex paraguariensis) as a precursor. A thorough characterization of the particles was carried out by HRTEM, XRD, FTIR, DLS, XPD, and static contact angle. The characterization indicated that the crystallinity, surface charge, and wettability depend on the synthesis parameters.

Carbon dots-doped materials, YMnAp@CD and YMFenAp@CD, showed amphiphilic properties with the capacity to adsorb Crystal violet (CV) and Oil Blue A (OBA) from aqueous or toluene-based solutions. The mentioned materials showed the highest number of adsorption sites, with values of 2.95 ± 0.05 and 3.18 ± 0.04 mmol CV/gads and 1.65 ± 0.02 0.97 ± 0.06 mmol OBA/gads on monocomponent systems, and through a multi-component analysis, the competition for adsorption sites was elucidated.

ce:section.ce:para: The increase in organic molecules in water bodies, including pesticides, dyes, pharmaceuticals, and personal care products, has caused widespread concern, due to the associated environmental and health risks.[1–4] Many of them are hard to eliminate from the water sources due to their high chemical stability. They might persist in the effluent even after traditional wastewater treatments.[5–7].

Compounds such as polymer degradation byproducts pesticides can have hydrophobic nature and still be present in aquatic media such as natural colloids. [8] They can affect aquatic biota but they might be also transported to solid systems such as ground soil, generating alternative contamination pathways which could lead to the poisoning of plants, insects, and microorganisms. [9–11].

Adsorption is a widely explored process for the removal of dissolved pollutants from a liquid or gaseous solvent. It has been exploited for wastewater remediation, but it is of interest for the remediation of organic matrixes. The selective adsorption of sulfur-containing compounds on fuels is a suitable example of a process of environmental interest. [12].

Its versatility is due to the adsorption mechanism, this is the interaction between the adsorbent surface and the pollutant molecules. Adsorption is suitable to eliminate both hydrophilic and lipophilic organic pollutants, from both aqueous and organic environmental matrixes. This implies great advantages over alternatives such as chemical oxidation, due to the high flexibility in terms of design and operation.[13–17] Adsorption is typically a reversible process, the adsorbent can be regenerated, significantly decreasing the cost of the treatment due to the potential reuses. These characteristics make adsorption the most promising method for large-scale applications. [18].

The design or selection of the adsorbent is a crucial factor for the whole process. A suitable solid to be applied as an adsorbent to the remediation of both water and organic solvents should have a high surface area, a low agglomeration degree when suspended in either of the solvents but also an amphiphilic surface which allows the interaction with both hydrophilic and hydrophobic molecules. [19] This is a high number of active sites for both polar and non-polar molecules. To the best of our knowledge, there is a lack of reports in the literature about the design of this kind of versatile material although the theory is quite clear.

There are plenty of reports in the literature regarding the adsorptive properties of hydroxyapatite (Ap), [Ca10(PO4)6(HO)2].[21–23] Ap is an interesting adsorbent due to its high chemical stability in a wide range of physicochemical conditions, high biocompatibility, strong affinity with ionic substances, and a working range in near-neutral pH.[24] However, crystalline pure, undoped and non-functionalized Ap materials show a relatively low surface area and adsorption sites.[23,24] Fe-doped Ap not only shows a higher surface area but also the concentration of O-containing surface groups with an enhanced surface negative charge.[24] The functionalization of the surface and the use of organic templates during the synthesis directly impact the adsorption performance.[25,26] Through this strategy Ap is a suitable support for the development of a more robust adsorbent.

Carbon dots (CD) are a relatively new carbon-based material with spherical-like geometry with a diameter lower than 10 nm. They have a core–shell structure with an sp2 shell surrounded by sp3 moieties, which depending on the precursors and the synthesis route can harbor functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, epoxy, aldehyde, amines, amides, etc.[25] This dual behavior results in an amphiphilic nature of the CD. Due to this, they can act as “surfactants” to stabilize Pickering emulsions, i.e., emulsions stabilized with solid materials.[19,20,28] CD have low toxicity, high biocompatibility, strong stability, and high photoluminescence. Their main limitation as adsorbents is their relatively low size. But supporting CD onto Ap-based structures might produce a versatile material able to simultaneously adsorb both hydrophilic and lipophilic molecules.

CD could be obtained through biomass such as agroindustrial wastes by relatively simple routes.[25] Ilex paraguariensis, popularly known as Yerba Mate (YM), is an important South American plant used traditionally in infusions,[29] producing more than 277 million kilos just in Argentina in 2019. Nowadays, there is no clear pathway to guarantee the reintroduction of wastes to the productive chain. Our group has previously reported its use as a feedstock to produce several kinds of materials, such as CD,[25] CD-decorated magnetite,[27] organic coated nano-magnetite,[26,30] or iron and copper oxide nanomaterials embedded on organic microparticles.[31] The physicochemical property of the aqueous extract of YM has potential application as a synthesis template for the obtaining of Ap-based materials.[24].

YM is a potential feedstock to produce CD but also as a synthesis template for Ap obtaining on serial processes. This study aims to study the resulting physicochemical properties of Ap-based materials doped with iron oxide or CD while using YM aqueous extract as a synthesis template over their performance as adsorbents for the simultaneous adsorption of hydrophilic and hydrophobic dyes from aqueous and toluene-based solutions, respectively.

Reactants. Calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)2) 95 % wt, ammonium dihydrogen phosphate (NH4H2PO4) 99 % wt, iron (II)sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O) 99 % wt, iron (III)nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O) 98 % wt, Oil Blue A (OBA) 98 % wt, and crystal violet (CV) 90 % wt were supplied by Sigma Aldrich. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) 99 % wt, potassium hydroxide (KOH) 99 % wt, sulfuric acid (H2SO4) 97 % wt, nitric acid (HNO3) 65 %, crystal violet (CV) and oil blue A (OBA) were provided by Merck. All were used without further purification. YM was from the commercial trademark “El Playadito” (Liebig, Farming Cooperative, Argentina, production lot 45–03). Deionized water was Milli-Q purified (18MΩ cm and 6 ppb TOC at 25 °C).
The procedure to obtain the YM aqueous extract is discussed in detail in our previous study. [31].

Synthesis of YM-template hydroxyapatite nanomaterials. An adaptation of the reported synthesis route by Mercado et al. was used.[23,24] Briefly, 5 g of Ca(OH)2 were dispersed into 40 mL of the YM extract, stirred, and heated to 80 °C. In parallel, 4.39 g of (NH4)H2PO4 were dissolved in 60 mL of H2O, and the pH was adjusted to 2.1 using H2SO4. This acid solution was added dropwise into the previous alkaline suspension over 2 h at 80 °C and 300 rpm. The resulting solid was separated by centrifugation, washed several times with deionized water, dried at 80 °C, and further calcined at 600 °C for 3 h under an air atmosphere. The resulting solid is denoted YMnAp. For purposes of comparison, a free YM material was obtained using pure water instead, which is denoted as nAp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of YM-template magnetic iron-doped hydroxyapatite nanomaterials.
ce:section.ce:para: The previous protocol was modified by dissolving FeCl2·4H2O and FeCl3·6H2O into the acidic solution, without any other change. The total amounts of iron and calcium ions were calculated to obtain an analytical molar ratio of Fe/Ca = 0.2 and an equal Fe(II)/Fe(III) ratio. The resulting solid was denoted as YMFenAp, and the YM-free analogous as FenAp.[23,24].
Synthesis of CD. The residual dry YM leaves of the extract preparation were calcinated in a closed vessel following the protocol discussed elsewhere.[25].

Preparation of the Ap-based material doped with CD. The YMnAP or YMFenAp powders (0.3 g) were suspended in 20 mL of CDs suspension, further diluted to 50 mL with water, and stirred for 30 min. The mixture was then transferred to a hydrothermal autoclave reactor and heated at 140 °C for 4 h. The autoclave was cooled to room temperature and the precipitates were washed several times with deionized water and dried at 80 °C for 24 h. The resulting solids were denoted as YMnAp@CD or YMFenAp@CD, depending on the Ap-based material used for each case.

To determine the name of a specific sample, only the abbreviations of the used precursors on the respective synthesis were considered. All abbreviations are nAp (nano-hydroxyapatite), YM (Yerba Mate), Fe (Iron containing sample), and CD (Carbon dots). For instance, the sample YMFenAp@CD was obtained using all precursors but the sample YMnAp is the sample in which only YM and nAp were used during its respective synthesis protocol.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) studies were carried out using a Tecnai F20 super twin TMP with a field emission of 0.1 nm in 200 Kv, equipped with a GATAN US 1000XP-P camera and an EDX Oxford instruments XMAX detector. The GATAN Digital Micrograph software was used to analyze the obtained images. Some drops of an aqueous suspension of the samples were deposited over copper-carbon grids.

Identification of the crystalline phases of all materials was assessed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using an Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku) equipped with a high-power tube PW337/10 LFF source with a Cu anode and a scintillator detector in Bragg-Brentano geometry.

The functional groups present in the prepared nanoparticles were identified by their IR spectrum, which was obtained with a Bruker IFS28 spectrophotometer equipped with a Globar source and a DTGS detector working at 128 scans with 4 cm−1 resolution in the overall range 4000–400 cm−1. Samples were prepared by pressing a ground mixture of 1% of the particle powder with 99% dry KBr, at a load of 5 tons.

N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms at 77 K were performed in an ASAP2020 Micromeritics model apparatus. Before the analysis, samples of ca. 0.2 g were degassed at a residual pressure of about 10−2 mbar at 393.15 K for 18 h. The corresponding BET-specific surface area (SBET) for all materials was computed using data within the linear range of relative pressures between 0.04 and 0.24.

Apparent static contact angle measurements were performed in a drop shape analyzer – DSA25E and evaluated by the Young-Laplace method using equipment and software from ADVANCE-KRÜSS. The powders were deposited on glass slides by adding droplets of aqueous suspensions and evaporating the solvent at 90 °C. All measurements were done at room temperature and in an air atmosphere following the experimental protocol explained by Rivera et. al.[32].

Electrophoretic mobility of the particles was measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a LiteSizer 500 by Anton Paar at (30.0 ± 0.1) °C, coupled to a Metrohm automatic titrator with an 867-pH module and an 846-dosing interface controlled by the software KalliopeTM. The setup involved a pH step of 0.5 pH, with an initial pH of 12 down to 1, with a pH-stabilization time of 5 min per step. Each measurement was done in triplicate, and they were performed on aqueous suspensions of the corresponding materials (0.15% w/w) with KNO3 as the background electrolyte. Smoluchowski approximation was used to carry out the process. HNO3 and NaOH solutions at 0.01 M were used to modify the pH.[16,25].

An XPS (X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy) spectrometer (XPS/ISS/UPS surface characterization platform, SPECS) equipped with a PHOIBOS 150 2D-DLD energy analyzer was used to analyze the surface chemistry of the samples. The pressure in the analysis chamber during the analysis was approximately 1 × 10−9 Pa. A monochromatized Al-Kα X-ray source (FOCUS 500) operated at 100 W was used for the measurements. The passing energy of the hemispheric analyzer was set at 100 eV for general spectra and 30 eV for high-resolution spectra. The surface charge compensation was controlled using a Flood Gun (FG 15/40-PS FG 500), operated at 58 μA and 2.5 eV.

For monitoring the dye concentration a Uv–Vis ShimadzuUV-2550 spectrophotometer was used. Adsorption studies were conducted in batch experiments. For each dye, variable mL-aliquots stock solutions were mixed with 5 mL of 100 ppm suspension of the respective solid and further diluted to a final volume of 25 mL, either with water with pH 6.7 ± 0.3 or toluene, according to the case. The suspensions were vigorously shaken in a Heidolph Unimax 1010 Shaker equipped with a Heidolph Incubator 1000 graduated to 25 ± 1 °C at 350 rpm for several hours until equilibration was achieved (preliminary result showed that under the studied conditions, the equilibrium is reached within 45 mins). After that time, the solid was removed by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 10 mins. In all cases, the amount of adsorbed dye was quantified according to equation (1):
(1) x/m = v × (Co − Ce)/m

Where x/m (mmol g−1) is the amount of dye moles adsorbed per g of particles; Co and Ce (mmol L‑1) represent the initial and equilibrium dye concentration in solution, respectively; v (L) is the solution volume used in the experiment; and m (g) is the mass of particles used. Data were expressed as the mean of three replicates. The adsorption Langmuir isotherm model was used to determine the adsorption isotherm parameters of the corresponding dye on the adsorbents. Equation (2) describes the mathematical expression for this model:
(2)

Where Qe (mmol g−1) is the equilibrium adsorption capacity, Ceq is the equilibrium adsorption concentration, Bmax (mmol g−1) is the maximum adsorption capacity, and kL (L mmol−1) is the affinity coefficient or Langmuir constant.[16,23,24].

For the simultaneous adsorption experiment, the experimental conditions and quantity of dyes are the same as previously discusses. Three systems were prepared: two of them correspond to the water and toluene solutions containing the required concentration of CV and OBA, respectively. The third system is a water-toluene-material mixture which was sonicated for 5 min before the simultaneous addition of the dyes solutions previously discussed. However, an increasing concentration of CV was added to the aqueous phase before mixing the three systems. The final suspension contained OBA in a concentration whereby the isotherm plateau is reached in the monocomponent adsorption isotherm for the corresponding material. Then, the system was shaken following the same protocol.

Scheme 1 summarizes all the syntheses for the obtaining of all materials. For each synthesis, the obtained material depends on the reagents used. For example, to obtain YMnAp, the addition of Fe salts is discarded. In total, 6 AP-based materials were synthesized and divided into two groups: nAp, YMnAp, YMnAp@CD, and their Fe-containing analogs FenAp, YMFenAp, and YMFenAp@CD. Pure CD were characterized in depth in our previous report.[25] Briefly, the CD contains a polyaromatic domain surrounded by aliphatic moieties with oxygen-containing functional groups ( Fig. S1 – Supporting information file),[25,27] which allow them to interact with polar molecules such as water through ion–dipole or intermolecular hydrogen bonding. Moreover, the aromatic core might interact with aromatic molecules through π-π stacking.

Particle characterization. Only the iron-doped materials evidenced magnetism at room temperature. For FenAp, a saturation magnetization of 4 emu g−1 has been previously reported.[24] In Videos S1 to S4 of the supporting information file (SI), the response of some selected materials exposed to a magnetic field can be observed.

Some HRTEM micrographs are shown in Fig. 1
, which confirm that the obtained materials are in the nanoscale. Due to the substantial agglomeration, it was not possible to obtain statistical size distribution. Nevertheless, pure hydroxyapatite has been reported to have a cylindrical-like structure with diameters between 100 and 150 nm with regular contour.[23,24] But when Fe or YM are introduced, the diameter decreases to 50–100 nm, and the morphology homogeneity is modified, resulting in more irregular geometry. Moreover, for YMnAp and YMFenAp, domains with lower optical density (relatively intense white spots) are observed on the surfaces, which are attributed to defects resulting from the organic matter calcination.

Regarding YMnAp@CD and YMFenAp@CD, the presence of globular lumps characterized by a lower optical density is discernible. These features have different higher optical densities than that observed for YMnAp and YMFenAp. They have a well-defined spherical-like geometry with a mean diameter of 4.1 ± 1.2 nm. Interestingly, free CD obtained through this route has a reported mean diameter of 5.1 ± 1.5 nm. These surface domains are attributable to surface CD.[25].

N2 adsorption isotherms ( Fig. S2 ) for all synthesized materials have type II isotherms according to the IUPAC classification, typical of non-porous or macroporous materials.[33] Due to the dimension and the morphology of the particles, and under what is observed in the HRTEM micrographs, the existence of microporous material is discarded. Table 1 summarizes the specific surface area for all materials, as determined by the BET model. Following the introduction of Fe, YM-as template, and CD, the surface area of the materials increases from 41 to 130 m2 g−1 for nAp and YMFenAp@CD, respectively.

X-ray diffractograms of all AP-based materials are shown in Fig. 2 . With crystal purity above 99%, AP (ICDD 9–432) is the crystalline material most likely to be present in the samples. However, due to the positions of the AP peaks, the maghemite/magnetite domains for Fe-containing materials could overlap one another, so the presence of at least one of these iron oxide phases should not be disregarded, which is also corroborated by the magnetic properties of the powder. Using YM or Fe cations in the synthesis does not shift the position of the peaks, suggesting that there is no modification to the AP crystalline structure; i.e., the introduction of the Fe cations into the crystalline cell is discarded. Moreover, the interplanar distance of 2.8 Å shown in Fig. S3 is in line with the theoretical value for the length of the plane (211), which is perpendicular to the c-axis, as can be observed in the XRD diffractogram at 31.77°. This reinforces the likelihood of the presence of the AP phase in all materials. Nevertheless, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of this last peak decreases systematically with the introduction of either Fe or YM to the synthesis, which indicates that the crystallinity of the materials decreases.

The peaks at 25.8° and 32.19° are attributed to the planes (002) and (121), respectively, and their relative ratio, I002/I112, is proportional to the ratio of the length parameters of the c-axis/a-axis.[34] The values for this parameter of 0.86, 0.80, and 0.50 for nAp, FenAp, and YMnAp, respectively, suggest inhibition of the c-axis following the introduction of Fe ions or YM as templates, which is in line with what is observed in the HRTEM micrographs. Otherwise, supporting CD on the surfaces does not seem to modify the crystalline structure.

Fig. 3 shows the IR spectra. Concerning the AP-based materials, the fundamental vibration modes of the PO4 −3 group from the AP structure are observable at 470, 560, 610, 960, and 1030–1100 cm−1.[35] Moreover, the broad band around 1640 and 3430 cm−1 corresponds to the δ(H–O–H) bands and ν(O–H) vibration modes. About the last peak, a major contribution is observed on the part of YMnAp@CD and YMFenAp@CD. This behavior may suggest an increase in hydrophilicity after supporting the CD over the materials.

nAp is the only sample that shows a sharp band at 3561 cm−1, which is attributable to hydroxyl groups in AP with a high degree of crystallinity. The absence of this peak for the other samples supports the likelihood that this results in a crystallinity decrease following the introduction of Fe cations or YM as a template, which is in agreement with the XRD data. Also, residual carbonate impurities may be present in the AP structure, evidenced by the broad peak in 1460 cm−1.

The CD spectrum is also shown; besides the characteristic absorption band of O–H mentioned above, the presence of the organic moieties is appreciable due to the vibration bands at 2915, 2818, and 1405 cm−1 of alkyl and aryl groups.[25] The peaks at 1717 and 1130 cm−1 strongly suggest the presence of carboxylic acid and other organic oxygen-containing functional groups.[36] The CD-doped materials show these organic bands, suggesting surface modification, but in addition to this, a contribution of the C = O band shifts to lower wavenumbers due to the interaction of some carboxylic groups with the iron or calcium ions. However, contributions of free C = O are still evidenced at 1717 cm−1.

TGA curves, shown in Fig. 4 , provide information relative to adsorbed substrates and decomposition temperatures. nAp shows a 4.3% total mass, attributed to water desorption (T ≤ 200 °C), dehydration of hydroxides (380 – 500 °C), and elimination of carbonate-like groups (500 – 800 °C). FenAp, YMnAp, and YMFenAp show a similar trend, with an increase in the mass loss in the region (75 – 200 °C), suggesting an enhancement in the amount of adsorbed water per gram of particles compared to the nAp. For Fe-containing samples, the dehydration of surface Fe-OH could also be considered. Thus, in agreement with the IR analysis, the surface concentration of –OH groups increases upon introducing Fe or YM to the synthesis. A higher mass loss is observable for YMnAp@CD and YMFenAp@CD in all the temperature ranges mentioned previously.

Nevertheless, the pyrolysis of the organic domains must be considered. Free CDs typically have a two-step degradation pattern for water desorption and organic matter combustion in the ranges of 50 – 200 and 200 – 500 °C, as well as in ranges even higher than this.[37] A rough estimation of the weight fraction of the CD on both materials was obtained, only considering the difference in total mass loss, with values of 20.17 and 19.33% for YMnAp@CD and YMFenAp@CD, respectively.

Researchers typically report the Z-potential value instead of the Electrophoretic mobility regardless of the morphology and the size of the material, but that is a common mistake. For further discussion, please analyze Section S1.1 and Fig. S4 . Electrophoretic mobility profiles according to pH are represented in Fig. 5 . The lowest pH value studied was around 4.0, due to the AP dissolution.[21] As reported in the literature, nAp has an isoelectric point (IEP) of 7.6.[38,39] However, FenAp and YMnAP show an IEP of 4.1 and 5.8, respectively. This behavior could be due to the increase in the –OH surface group concentration previously discussed.[23] In the studied pH range, the IEP for YMFenAp is not shown; however, more negative µe values are observed. Both these behaviors may be due to the synergetic contribution of Fe and YM. A further decrease in this parameter is observed for both CD-doped materials due to the additional presence of the surface organic group. Similar results were reported for magnetite nanoparticles when the surface was modified with anionic humic-like substances.[40].

The survey XPS spectra of some samples are shown in Fig. S5
. All samples show the main bands corresponding to hydroxyapatite materials: Ca, O, P. [41] Only FenAp, YMFenAp, and YMFenAp@CD show Fe contribution. C peaks were also observable in all samples, which is attributed to the adventitious carbon used as an internal standard at 284.8 eV, to correct binding energy due to the in-situ charging effect. In the same figure, high-resolution spectra of C1s signals for YMFenAp and YMFenAp@CD are shown. Besides the previously mentioned signal at 284.8 eV, the first sample shows a contribution centered at 288.1 eV, attributed to carbonates, which is coherent with the FTIR analyses. Nevertheless, YMFenAp@CD shows an additional species at 281.4 eV due to the presence of C = C/C–C bonds due to the graphitic structure of the CD. Thus, this new peak suggests the presence of the CD on the surface of this material. FenAp@CD shows the same contribution (spectra not shown).

The surface stoichiometric ratio was determined for all materials, considering the sensitivity factors relative to the different elements Ca:P:O:Fe, the results of which are shown in Table 1. The theoretical stoichiometry is Ca1P0.6O2.4O0.2. Thus, all samples have non-stoichiometric surfaces. Nevertheless, the P/Ca ratio is in line with several reports, and it is attributed to surface acid phosphates.[41] For P/Ca and O/Ca, ratios are also higher. Kandori et. al.[42] suggested that this is due to the formation of surface defects, producing an increase in –OH groups, which results in a more hydrophilic surface compared to pure nAp.

The wettability of the materials was determined by the apparent static contact angle (SCA) measurements shown in Table 1 (for the photographs, see table S1). This parameter is modulated by the chemical composition and the morphology structure.[32,43] The materials studied here have similar textural properties (vide supra). Nevertheless, this data is for qualitative comparison due to the non-homogeneous deposition of the materials on the glass slides. [44,45] nAp exhibits an SCA of (43.1 ± 0.3)° and (47.4 ± 0.5)° for water and toluene droplets, respectively, suggesting slightly hydrophilic properties. This could be explained by the possible formation of H-bonds of the surface hydroxyl groups with water molecules.[46] The SCA for FenAp, YMnAp, and YMFenAp is lower for water but significantly higher for toluene, suggesting that the particles are even more hydrophilic than nAp, which may be due to the increased concentration of the surface-OH groups,[47] in accordance with the FTIR, TGA and XPS analyses (vide supra).

Interestingly, CD-doped nanocomposites have relatively low SCA for both solvents, suggesting amphiphilic properties of the particles. The accepted structure of the CD is composed of a quantum-sized graphitic-like core surrounded by aliphatic chains with oxygen-containing terminal groups[48]. This special feature enables the CD to interact with water through H-bonds and aromatic molecules through π – π stacking.[48].

Overall, the obtained hydroxyapatite (nAp) is pure crystalline with a cylindrical-like morphology. But after doping it with iron oxide domains (FenAp) the resulting morphology is more irregular and its diameter decreases and the appearance of iron oxide crystalline phases took place. The increase in the –OH group surface concentration is also observable. Additionally, for the individual use of YM (YMnAp), the decreases in the sizes are also observable, without the appearance of a new crystalline phase in the material but with a marked increase in surface area. With the simultaneous use of Fe and YM (YMFenAp), the synergy of both individual processes takes place increasing both –OH group surface concentration and surface area. All those three composite materials show hydrophilic behavior, due to the relatively high surface area and –OH group concentration. Nevertheless, upon supporting the Carbon Dots (CD) on YMnAp (YMnAp@CD) and YMFenAp (YMFenAp@CD), the composites developed an amphiphilic nature, due to the amphiphilic structure on the CD containing simultaneously a graphitic-like core surrounded by polar functional groups. Moreover, those two composites developed a relatively higher surface area.

Mono-component dye adsorption experiments. Fig. 6 shows the experimental isotherms obtained for adsorption and desorption experiments at 25 °C, and Table 2 summarizes all the fitting parameters of the monocomponent adsorption. In all cases, the one-adsorption site Langmuir model fits with R2 > 0.95. These suggest that, concerning the adsorption of both dyes, all materials have a quasi-homogeneous surface. For CV adsorption (Fig. 6 top), all materials show affinity towards the adsorption of this dye, with the maximum number of sites, Bmax, ranging from 0.42 ± 0.02 up to 3.18 ± 0.04 mmol g−1 for nAp and YMFenAp@CD, respectively. Similar CV maximum adsorption uptake has been reported for a composite of hydroxyapatite/magnetite/bentonite under comparable conditions; however, the affinity coefficient values, KL, obtained here for CD-doped materials are higher,[49] which suggest a stronger adsorbate-adsorbent interaction. Due to the cationic nature of the CV and net negative charge surfaces of most of the materials, an electrostatic mechanism is strongly suggested. Indeed, an experimental relation ( Fig. S6 ) between the electrophoretic mobility of the particle suspension at pH = 7 and the Bmax for CV adsorption support this hypothesis.[16] For nAp, although the net charge is positive at the pH used, the surface PO4 −3 groups may contribute the most to CV adsorption.[24] However, for the other materials, the dehydroxylated -O− and COO− groups may be responsible for the adsorption of CVs.

Different behavior is observed for OBA adsorption (Fig. 6 Bottom); CD-doped materials are still the most active adsorbents. However, with the highest KL, nAp shows a bigger adsorption uptake than FenAp, YMnAp, and YMFenAp but with KL almost the same for nAp and FenAp. These results may be due to different adsorption mechanisms for the adsorption processes of the two dyes. For YMnAp@CD and YMFenAp@CD, due to the relatively high concentration of sp2-C in CD,[50] π-π stacking between the dye and the aromatic organic groups is suggested. However, for nAp and FenAp, some studies suggest complexion between positively charged surface Ca2+ ions on the hydroxyapatite structure with the amine groups on the dye.[22,51] In this regard, the complexation of Ca2+ ions with organic ligands containing amine and aromatic groups has been widely reported.[52] Nevertheless, although Fe cations may also contribute as adsorption sites through the formation of metal complexes, the more hydrophilic nature of the Fe-containing materials (vide supra) results in a decrease in the effective surface available, i.e., the available interface area in contact with the toluene phase.[24,53,54]

Reusability tests were done for YMnAp@CD and YMFenAp@CD and the results are shown in Fig. S7 . There is a slight decrease in the Bmax parameter for both dyes on mono-component systems. Nevertheless, the maximum decay is around 13% for the 5th cycle. Thus, these materials show a high reuse potential.

Bi-component dye adsorption experiments. During the preliminary test for the bi-component adsorption, it was evidenced that both CD-doped materials were able to stabilize toluene-water emulsions in the absence of the dyes for more than 24 h confirming the amphiphilic nature evidenced in the SCA results (vide supra). Fig. S8 shows some photographs of the water/toluene systems containing YMnAP and YMnAP@CD. The phase separation of the YMnAP-containing system is evident, with the particles suspended in the aqueous phase confirming its more hydrophilic nature. For YMnAP@CD, the formation of a stable emulsion for at least 24 h is observed. This behavior is also obtained using YMFenAP@CD, which could be due to the synergetic effect of the intrinsic hydrophilic behavior of the AP-structure due to the –OH groups in its surface,[54] induced due to the introduction of iron and CD domains, and the amphiphilic properties of the CD(vide supra) on the surface.[55]

However, CV and OBA are selectively soluble in water and toluene, and upon their introduction to the systems, the obtained emulsion was only stable for around 2 h and the phase separation takes place. This result might be due to the occupancy of the active sites on the surface of the particles due to the adsorption of the dyes. The just discussed, was the reason why only CD-doped materials were considered for bi-components experiments. Fig. 7 shows the corresponding adsorption isotherms.

Similar results were obtained for both particles; CV maximum adsorption uptake was lower than in monocomponent systems even in the absence of OBA, which could be attributed to competitive toluene adsorption due to the possibility of the π-π interaction. The total number of adsorbed moles, Bmax,total, was calculated as the sum of the Bmax for each dye at a respective equilibrium concentration of OBA, and interestingly each material follows a different behavior. For YMFenAp@CD, this parameter follows an increasing trend; higher Bmax,total values are observed for higher values of OBA concentration at the equilibrium, ranging from 2.223 ± 0.009 to 2.505 ± 0.003 mol/g, which suggest that both dyes molecules share the same adsorption sites, though, under the conditions studied on the bicomponent adsorption isotherms, the surface shows somewhat higher adsorption affinity for OBA than for CV.[23] Regarding YMnAp@CD, Bmax,total remained constant at 2.992 ± 0.010 mol/g, which suggest that competitive adsorption between both dyes take place for all the OBA concentration range.

Thus, for both systems, the dyes show a competitive adsorption nature. This hypothesis might be considered the opposite of the different mechanisms proposed for the monocomponent systems (vide supra). However, regarding the adsorption of both dyes, the nanocomposites have quasi-homogeneous surfaces, i.e., the adsorption sites are well-dispersed around the surfaces, and due to steric hindrance, the adsorption sites are not independent.

This study evidences the versatility of residual Ilex paraguariensis to be used as a template and raw material for obtaining amphiphilic nanocomposites of carbon dots supported on iron oxide-doped hydroxyapatite. The results suggest that using the organic template, a change in the morphology due to the inhibition of the c-axis concerning the a-axis length is evidenced. On introducing Fe and the YM, surface chemistry changes due to the promotion of the hydrophilicity nature of the nanocomposites with respect to the pure AP structure are also evidenced. Furthermore, supporting the CD, obtained from YM, over the AP-based materials mentioned, results in new amphiphilic properties of the nanocomposites, which can stabilize water/toluene Pickering emulsion. All the doping processes studied here result in an increase in the negative surface charge when in aqueous suspensions at pH 7.0.

Promising adsorption properties for CV and OBA removal from water and toluene, respectively, were evidenced for the nanocomposites. Due to the cationic nature of the CV, an increase in the surface charge of the materials enhances the number of adsorption sites, suggesting an electrostatic adsorption mechanism. Nevertheless, regarding OBA adsorption, a π-π interaction mechanism is proposed. However, competitive adsorption processes are evidenced for the bi-component system.

In general, the approach considered in this study opens a remarkably interesting landscape for the use of agro-industrial residues in series processes for obtaining more than one material with interesting adsorbent properties.
